vocab.meaning: beloved (p.p. of amar used as adj.)

grammar.title: Imperative ‘imiten’
grammar.detail: ustedes-imperative of imitar. -ar verbs swap the vowel: imita → imiten. Used here without an explicit subject pronoun, very common in Spanish commands.

grammar.title: ‘como’ + noun
grammar.detail: ‘como hijos muy amados’ — ‘as (very) beloved children’. ‘como’ introduces the role/capacity in which the action is done.

expression.phrase: imiten a Dios, como hijos muy amados
expression.note: The basis for imitation is family identity, not duty. Spanish ‘muy amados’ intensifies — ‘dearly loved’ children.

vocab.meaning: to lead a life of, to live a life of
to give oneself up, to surrender

grammar.title: Imperative ‘lleven’
grammar.detail: Another ustedes-imperative chained to v.1: imiten… y lleven. The ‘y’ keeps both commands under the same audience.

grammar.title: ‘así como’ + clause
grammar.detail: ‘just as / in the same way that’. Sets up a comparison: live a life of love, just as Christ loved us.

expression.phrase: ofrenda y sacrificio fragante para Dios
expression.note: OT sacrificial language — the smoke of acceptable offerings ‘pleased God’s nostrils’. Paul applies it to Christ’s self-giving.

grammar.title: Impersonal ‘debe mencionarse’
grammar.detail: deber + infinitive (passive reflexive) = ‘should not even be mentioned’. The ‘se’ marks an impersonal/passive construction.

grammar.title: ‘ni siquiera’
grammar.detail: ‘not even’ — strong negation. Always paired with another negative element (here ‘no es propio’ implicit).

expression.note: A Hebrew-style absolute prohibition — not just ‘don’t practise it’ but ‘don’t even let it be named among you’. The point is communal reputation and culture.
